title: User top-level globals emitted as raw C symbols — (define free 0) shadows libc free, SIGBUS at teardown
goal: Found by the SICP ch5 corpus (PR #109), verified. A top-level (define free 0) emits a global with the raw symbol name `free`, which shadows/overrides the libc function at JIT/AOT link; the program prints correctly then dies with fatal SIGBUS at teardown (arena/runtime calls free() which now resolves to the user's data). `(define time 5)` happens to survive on this platform but the collision class is generic: any user global named after a linked C symbol (free, malloc, exit, system, sin, ...) can silently corrupt runtime calls. Verified: (define free 0)(display (+ free 1)) -> prints 1 then SIGBUS, both -r and AOT.
minimal_repro: (define free 0) (display (+ free 1))  ;; prints 1, then fatal SIGBUS at exit
root_cause_hint: User globals should be name-mangled (e.g. eshkol_user_ prefix or module-qualified) or given internal/private linkage so they can never collide with C runtime symbols. Check how codegenVariableDefinition/global emission names GlobalVariables and how the JIT/AOT symbol tables resolve; the SICP corpus GC register `free` is the canonical trigger.
acceptance: (define free 0) works and process exits cleanly; same for malloc/exit/time/system/sin as variable names; libc calls unaffected; -r AND AOT; no symbol-resolution regression for extern/FFI declarations (which SHOULD bind C symbols)
campaign_payoff: Closes a whole namespace-collision crash class; 4th bug surfaced by the SICP gate.
